speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we came back from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and then--

speaker: interviewee
text: From that [NON-ENGLISH] summer vacation.

speaker: interviewer
text: --right. And now-- and now we're gonna have-- I just want you to tell me all the details from here on out-- all your war experiences, how things changed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what some of the laws were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how you felt, what it was like for your parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what it was like for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- are you sitting back? Are you comfortable?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when we traveled to my hometown, in 19-- 1939, this was the middle of September-- almost the end. 'Cause when the Germans marched into this town-- to this town, Garki-- it must have been early September year-- days, because this is where I even heard people say that Warsaw is still fighting. This was in the beginning of September. The capital of Poland, Warsaw-- for some reason, there were a lot of patriots that didn't want to give up this-- the capital, to the Germans, which is probably in the history of how Poland was defeated. And there was a lot of killings. And the Germans were stronger. And they eventually had to give in. And they took a lot of Polish army as prisoners of war. And that was a very strenuous time for all the residents. We didn't know where to do or where to run. People-- what I saw as a child, people were grabbing belongings and trying to run away. Money had no value. I saw banknotes, paper money, rolling in the gutter in the-- in the cities, because people were trying to buy-- exit, going, running. Some people came back later on after the law said-- they thought there is nothing they can do about it. But whoever had relatives overseas tried to get out somehow. We did have relatives, but it was impossible to get in touch or to get any access-- train, ship. It was impossible. But I realized then, as a child, that the money doesn't buy it-- that people were trying to do something. The Polish money was devalued, totally. There was really, literally, money strolling on the streets, and nobody would worry about it, because the German mark was important. And also, the-- the wheeling and dealing started of-- with food. And because we had coal mines-- this is one interesting situation. My mother had a brother living in a bigger city somewhere, not far where Jack was born, in Lodz. And they established a ghetto much earlier than where I lived, 'cause smaller towns were in touch with non-Jewish farmers. We could deal something. We could manage to get a little bit of food. But what we did have-- coal to heat, to-- to bring heat. And a piece-- a brick of coal looks like a piece of diamond. It sparkles. I don't know if you ever saw coal coming out from the coal mines, from underneath.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we got a hold of something like that, I don't know how my mother tried to send it to her brother, at least to keep warm. Because if you hungry and cold, at least-- well, one thing we did have is heat in the house. It was a big fireplace, and they put as much coal as they could to keep it warm all night.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I met my parents, when we arrived, my father was an optimist. I think maybe he didn't want to show it to me. 'Cause I remember later on, later on when food was a lot scarce and he-- there wasn't enough to eat, he used to say, you go ahead and eat. I already ate. I'm positive he wasn't eating. He was either fasting or leaving the food for his child. I'm positive, because he always used to say, well, I'm not hungry. I'll eat later. Go ahead. There wasn't that much, but this was what stuck in mind about my parents. But we stayed in the same place, in the same big building, because it was on the Jewish side. But then came laws that the big main streets, like where a promenade is, or a-- where the street I used to go buy-- Jews are not allowed to walk. It was the first thing. They-- this wasn't a ghetto We had-- but they separated the Jewish people from the gentiles. And they started to-- the neighbors, if there were any non-Jewish neighbors, they wouldn't even come to see us. That was like totally divided, we. And there was a Jewish committee. Well, this Jewish committee, I'm not so proud about, because there, most of them were collaborators. They claim, some of them, that after second world war talked about it, that they tried to protect us by sending out as many people to work-- to labor camps, to work to produce, to support the industry of the Nazis, in order for us to survive. In some ways, it's true, because I spoke to a lot of people from my hometown. But these were only the youngsters. They took away the disabled, the old people, the ones that were-- first of all, they started to sift out to make like lists.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is on welfare? Who is poor? Also who is a criminal? Also who has a bad record in town? So these people were the first one to get rid of.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they took people which were over 50 or over 40. In those days, this were-- that mean old. So what was left is the able ones, the healthy ones. And this Jewish committee which-- he was the leader, the president. His name was Mr. Merrin-- Moniek Merrin. And he was known in-- in the history of collaborators, because he was destroyed at the end from the Nazis. Just like all the ghettos had a president, he was the king. And he was the one that separated me from my parents. He was the-- if the transport wasn't enough, he wanted to do better. He wanted to do more. If there was that many people, he wanted to supply more-- more help, more Jews. And he kept on saying-- there was another trick that they did in the beginning, which was '40, '41. They would take young men, men in their early 20s or 18, 19-year-old, 20-year-olds, in the early 20s-- capable men-- send them to a labor camp-- not a concentration-- there was no concentration camp-- and send them home for a special holiday, or send the wives of the family a check, a paycheck. So this was like a cover up. This, I even heard when I was home.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see? They're alive. They're working. They're providing. And the wives get paid by the Germans. So it's not so bad, is it? It's not so bad, because they're doing a job. And this way, they'll survive. And the wives get some kind of a pension. It was such ideas that they brought out ideas. And I remember distinctly where the Jewish committee was. And they were employing men, which I used to have friends-- we used to-- my mother used to say, he is a policeman now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were scared of them. They were wearing white hats-- Jewish boys-- with stars-- with-- this was only in my hometown. I don't know if it was anywhere else. A white band-- it would say [NON-ENGLISH] or something like that-- with a hat, like a sailor's hat, with the thing here, and it-- and the Jewish star. And they were supposed to help a transport-- or barge into in the middle of night in a building and knock in the doors. And we were shocked, because some of those boys joined to survive. They-- they didn't think of them being collaborators or helping the Nazis. They were thinking that is a way of surviving. This is a way I'm gonna manage through the war if-- if-- if I do that, or if I-- I'm not doing it for the Nazi. I'm doing it for the-- the Jewish committee. And the Jewish committee had meetings. They were contact. They lived like kings. They had everything. They had access to transport to go places. But one thing I remember about-- in the beginning of the war-- because they were always picking on male-- on-- on-- on men, on Jewish men like my father. A beard, or glasses, or looking Jewish was too much for them, especially-- they did it for fun. Some of the soldiers, the German soldiers, they would make fun. They would pick or hurt. So we had-- this, I remember as a child. My father had a close friend, a doctor, a Jewish doctor. He was still allowed to practice. So he made a certificate out that my father, health-wise, is not able to stand in the line for a ration, for a bread or potatoes. And I would be able, because I-- I volunteered. | said to my father, they're gonna hit you. They gonna to break your glasses. My father was wearing heavy glasses. I have a picture here. And I-- I don't think it's safe for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave me the certificate. And I would stand for hours in the line for my father and my mother. And I was so proud when I could come home with some rations, some food, some tea, sometimes dry goods to cook, you know, something like a-- a noodles-- something that a ration was allowed.

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to say we're gonna have butter today. Everybody was standing for hours. And I'll never forget how hard it was to be in the line with all these people, and standing there and waiting to be helped. And they came over. They screamed at me sometimes. [NON-ENGLISH]. What are you doing here-- in German. I would say, my father is sick. And then I was afraid to say sick, because then they started to get rid of sick people. And so you-- you didn't know what to do; and the scares, the fear-- continuously, continuously living in that fear. And that was from the beginning of '40, '41. But in the midst of everything, I still had a group of Jewish girls-- what I'm trying to remember. And we met in a home illegally-- because that was very dangerous-- as a group of Zionist movement, to give us hope, to do something, to learn Hebrew songs, to get together-- risking, literally, our life. I was afraid to tell my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember one day, I came home, and my mother was so worried. Why are you going? I said, I'm not going far. We're not talking about miles away. It was down the street, maybe, somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met some people, and I learned about a socialist Zionist organization. And I never heard it in all my life, because my father wouldn't allow to talk like that-- that anthropology-- not about seven days that God created each day. There were so many interesting subjects-- and the right of a girl that is equal-- stuff like that. But then-- [SIGHS] --they-- they were afraid to meet. We'd-- we'd-- these pictures that I kept through my camp, these are the pictures of that group that we had. We had a-- a leader-- like one girl, which was older, and she knew more. And she was, what in Hebrew it's called [HEBREW]. She was the head of that group of the [HEBREW]. So she was-- she was-- I don't-- oh, I'm sure she doesn't live. I'm sure, because then we split. We all went our-- our own ways, unfortunately. And so in 1942, they started to talk about-- oh, no, no, no, no. Before that, they sent all the residents of this town, which is the famous town-- Auschwitz. It was called Oswiecim in Polish. It's still called Oswiecim. German called it the Auschwitz. Oswiecim was only a few kilometers from where I grew up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what they did, in the beginning before they start to build this place, they took all the Jews, and they-- people used to come into knock in the door, and say, whoever can afford, has room to accommodate, to share your little space that you have with the people-- like immigrants from this town-- into Bedzin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I do remember that a Jewish committee tried to locate, tried to make room for these Jews, which was only temporary, unfortunately. Because then they were all transported. But at that time, I remember I met new people, people that never lived in this town. They came from Auschwitz, into Bedzin, into Sosnowiec-- and so all these Jewish little towns around us to settle. And little did we know-- nobody said anything, nobody understood why they are transporting all these people out. They started to build the destroying-- the camp to destroy. The program was how to-- the final solution-- what to do with all these Jews. So eventually, they started to send them out. But I didn't hear about Auschwitz until I left home. And even then, when I was in camp, there were some women coming in from Auschwitz to work. They were scared to talk about it. They said, there are kindergartens, that Auschwitz has-- has homes. They were afraid to tell us the truth. They had head shaved for hygiene. They wear uniform, because we had no way of changing clothes and stuff. That was the excuse. But never did they talk about what's really-- all crematoriums and stuff like that. We did find out later on in the camps, but it wasn't-- later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1942-- [SIGHS] there were rumors about a ghetto. The ghetto was supposed to be on a field on a-- outside that street where I lived, outside those buildings. Those buildings were later on occupied by the non-Jewish resident-- by the Poles-- whoever took over.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I wasn't dumb anymore. So what happened is around May-- it was after Passover, 1942-- they-- there was a law to gather all the Jews into one point, and to-- they call it in German a [GERMAN], like a gathering. And this was an orphanage. It used to be an orphanage-- a Jewish orphanage-- but the kids were gone by then. And this was very close to a train station. And so they gathered all-- my father used to work in a factory then, for the Germans. They used to have-- it-- it-- it's probably-- well, it doesn't exist now. But probably a lot of people with the last transport that left Bedzin were still working for this company. It was called Rossner-- Rossner Factories. It was like-- like Schindler had the production. That-- that was production in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So since my father worked, they let my parents go home. And they tried to sort out only young kids, young girls, with little hands, with-- because I had to work with my fingers-- put it in into the machine. And-- and they taught us how to do that. So I was never go-- I went never home. I-- that was it. So I-- I was taken away. And in all my years, I tried to remember the picture. It's fading away after 50 years, but I can still see my mother's face, my father's face, when they waved. I was taken away, and she was standing like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had pierced ears as a child-- gold earrings. So right away, they told me to take-- a little-- a little girl's pierced ears-- take out the earrings, and a little necklace. And I was on a transport-- on a train going somewhere. Well, that wasn't too far. It was a few kilometers from Bedzin. We were stopped in a big selection place. Somehow-- I don't know how-- without telephone, without mail-- somebody must have contacted my aunt that lived in that town, my mother's sister, my oldest sister-- which is not-- I don't know what happened then to her. But she came to the gates, to the-- we were all behind-- that this was called in-- in short, in German, [GERMAN]. [GERMAN]-- transferring camp-- transferring-- that we were only there to transfer to another camp. And she came to that gate. And she looked at me. And she said, your parents are OK. They're home. Try to be OK. Try to support. Try to survive-- something like that, she said. I haven't seen her anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they gave us those, like a tin bowl, a-- a bowl to have soup in, you know? It's made out-- it's like a tin, like aluminum-- a bowl. Each one was holding a bowl, and then they were like serving some kind of food which looked like soup. And to eat it like that-- that was my first experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had such nice hair when I came to the camp. And they said, [GERMAN], which means against lice-- against lice. They gonna shave it and-- go to camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put us on a train, and we traveled and traveled. Some girls started to hum a song and tried to be cheerful. And some were nasty. Some were picking. And you tried to huddle, and nothing-- no clothes, no nothing. We stayed in that place for-- who knows how long? I don't remember how many days we traveled. And then we finally came to a brand new camp. That was really a blessing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought this was a relief, because there were clean blankets. The wood-- the wood-- the barracks smelled like nobody lived there. They built it. They just built it. And it wasn't barbed wired yet. And it was a camp, a-- a-- a working camp. It was production. And so we were given numbers, not tattooed, just numbers. And they said that once we settled, you'll be allowed every two weeks to write home-- which we did. I used to get every two weeks. But what was so interesting-- my father had a sister in Belgium. That's the one that later on, I found and went to. This sister was in hiding. The Belgian gentiles made a-- bless their heart. They weren't as mean! I don't know why-- why. I'm not saying all the Poles-- because I-- I met very nice Pollacks too. But they were so jealous of the Jews that wherever they saw a Jew, they were reporting. My uncle, my aunt survived in hiding. He risked his life to go to the post office to [NON-ENGLISH], to send a package to me, to camp. And the post office knew that he's Jewish. She pretends she doesn't know him-- Mr. Gold, my uncle. I told my aunt and uncle after the war that God gave them a lot of years, because they-- that's how I survived. That's how they helped me. I have a picture here of them. There is one picture. My uncle-- my father, actually, my father wrote to me. Every two weeks, I was allowed to write a few words. And this is only until 1943. Then everything stopped, because they took all the Jews, and they didn't want us to know what happened to our family. So their last postcard-- I think I have it on there-- came back. No more Jews. And we didn't know. We'd-- I mean, we'd-- we were hoping they're alive. But nobody knew. They closed mail.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from 1942 to '43, which is a whole year-- working. And I'll explain what we were doing, what was the production. Since we had little fingers, this was a factory that produced spools of thread, raw thread-- not polished, not black, but raw, like greenish color from flax.

speaker: interviewee
text: The flaxes went-- we used to get like a-- a-- a rubber apron. It was like a-- a big apron, a heavy apron, to protect from the dirt, from the-- it was like mud coming out from the flaxes. And it went through spindles. And it went down into a little thing. Well, what was our job is to see that it doesn't tangle. And you're not supposed to stop the machine. There are 60 spools. I could never do it now. I could never work three years as a little-- as a young child. It was devastating-- the transmissions going, going. But this was like 60 spools on little-- on, like-- you know, I'm trying to describe-- like a-- a-- a pin, and the spools were all lined up. And the spools had to go up and down, up and down, even like you see a spool of thread-- even. But when it start to bulk, when it start to tangle, we're not supposed to stop the machine. But you were given like a piece of leather, like a cover, whatever. And some of the girls, when they were not careful, it chopped off a finger. But they were still working. They healed it up and worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to push it like I'm showing you now-- reel between the spindles, and try to pull that thread into that hole so that the spool goes again up and down, up and down, symmetrically. And every hour, 60 spools came out full, you know? The machine would fill them up, like, little bulky spools. So what you did, the machine stopped-- quick on a tray. The tray had the same things as the machine, those things. Put it on quick. Put empty ones on, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, it was interesting. It was fun. I mean, fun-- it was interesting to learn. It was a trade. I mean, my God, what an-- a-- what an exciting thing. You-- you wore wooden shoes. That's another story, because I had such a small foot. I still do. And they gave me those big-- I used to lose them walking to work, those wooden shoes. I had to go barefoot, because the-- the shoes were always-- they used to do it on purpose. A big foot, they used to give a little wooden shoe. And a-- a little foot, they used to drag their feet to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I learned it. I mastered it. And there was also a lift, which is another, a specialty. Whoever could master this way, because the spools were going like this-- 60 spools, 60 minutes. Every time, the machine had to be full. You take quick off the full ones. And they went to a polishing, to color. From this thread, they made uniforms, OK? So that's why our job was important.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the flax, from the field, whatever, came down that thread. The thread was fine, then made into spools, and then going into a polishing. And from all these threads, the factories used to make uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was called a [GERMAN]-- fine spindle whatever. And the firm was [GERMAN]. And this was an [GERMAN]-- like a labor camp. There were 1,200 women there. And they used to give us a little ration. So what I want to tell you about-- my parents; so they were still home. They were-- then they had a ghetto. They-- I-- I found out through a few words.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, the mail was going like that. I didn't speak German. They didn't speak German. So what we did, they wrote the Latin alphabet in Yiddish words, that looked-- I have those cards-- that looked like German.

speaker: interviewer
text: They would say-- they didn't spell it German right. But it would say, dear daughter, or [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was-- it was written like they spoke. So that was-- so that-- control, they were, you know, checking if we're spying, or-- what spying? We-- I was a-- I was a political prisoner. I-- I didn't understand the word political. But Jewish is a political prisoner. I was a political-- wear-- to wear that sign-- political prisoner-- because we were Jewish. What is a child political? That was it. So my father wrote to me once. Because you're from a religious home, and because you never ate nothing which isn't kosher, this is survival. This is very important. We don't want you to-- anything that was given to you to eat-- I'll never forget the way he explained it to me in this mail.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eat anything you can get. Whatever you receive, it's important. And don't worry. Because my upbringing was different, you know what-- kosher. And one time, I received a few-- I've got the postcards here. My aunt gave them to me back after the war when I came to Belgium-- that she heard from my parents. And she's gonna try to help me, which they did. And they were in Belgium. They were all the time in hiding, somewhere in a basement. So they came out once in a while. They would send me a little package. The package consist of stuff which I didn't really need it, you know, but I'm-- excuse me. I could make food out. I could sell it or-- or give it to people that were in the-- in a position to give me a-- a piece of bread, or a bowl of soup, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because she would send me-- sometimes it was something to wear, which I wasn't allowed to. Or-- well, what was a good part was bouillons, you know? I could always get a hold of somewhere hot water, and that was like a soup to us. Well, basically, she helped me to have my strength to-- to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I memorized in my head the address where they are. Because there was an atlas on the postcards, which I still have-- to be able-- if the war is over. And I have little notes from friends, in Polish, that wrote me on toilet paper, on anything you could hold on. We'll be-- soon, we'll be free. Soon, we'll be free. We wish you were-- like on a happy birthday. Today is your birthday. You'll be with your family-- together-- a lot of hope. A lot of hope was important-- was very important. I also remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we should stop here.
